content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807
428 J Street
• Sacramento. CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660
Fax (916) 322-0886
August 12, 1999
Lori J. Barker
Assistant City Attorney
City of Chico
411 Main Street
Post Office Box 3420
Chico, California 95927
Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-99-211
Dear Ms. Barker:
This letter is in response to your request for advice on behalf of Councilmember Coleen
Jarvis regarding the conflict ofinterest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the “Act”).’
QUESTIONS
1. May Councilmember Jarvis participate in decisions regarding the CDBG annual
budget, which includes a line item for the fair housing seminars?
2. May Councilmember Jarvis sign the contract on behalf of Legal Services for the
provision of the seminars?
3. May Councilmember Jarvis participate in decisions regarding the provision of funds to
Legal Services within the community organization funding process?
4. May Councilmember Jarvis sign the community organization funding agreement on
behalf of Legal Services?
5. Would the answer to question 3 change if the funding application from Legal Services
did not include an amount to be used for payment towards Councilmember Jarvis’s salary?
Government Code sections 81000 - 91014. Commission regulations appear at title 2, sections 18109
-
18995, of the California Code of Regulations.


File No. A-99-2 11
Page 2
CONCLUSIONS
1. Councilmember Jarvis may not participate in the decision to approve funding for a
contract between the city and Legal Services for fair housing seminars. However, other aspects
of the budget may be segmented in order to allow her to participate as to those items, as long as
the decisions are not interrelated with the line item for fair housing seminars, and the procedures
set forth in this letter are followed.
2. The conflict of interest provisions ofthe Act only prohibit public officials from
making, participating in making, or using their official position to influence a decision. They do
not prohibit the councilmember from signing a contract on behalf ofLegal Services. However,
other laws outside of the Act, such as Government Code Section 1090, might apply. We cannot
advise on this law since our jurisdiction is limited to the Act.
3. No. She may not participate in the decision to distribute funds to Legal Services.
4. See conclusion number 2.
5. No. The councilmember has a conflict in the decision because her source ofincome is
directly involved in the decision, and it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have some
financial effect on her source of income. The fact that the funding is used for her salary would
have been relevant ifthe only materiality standard that pertained to your question was the “nexus
test” as addressed below.
FACTS
The Legal Services ofNorthern California (“Legal Services”) is a nonprofit corporation.
It has an executive office in Sacramento, eight branch offices, and two program offices in various
locations. One ofthe branch offices is located in the City of Chico (the “city”). Councilmember
Jarvis is employed by Legal Services as the managing attorney of the Chico office. As such, she
is the highest level employee in the Chico office. Her income from Legal Services within the
past 12 months has exceeded $250. The total annual budget for Legal Services is approximately.
$5,700,000. The annual budget for the Chico branch office is approximately $500,000. There
are two matters considered annually by the city council that affect Legal Services.
Contract to Conduct Fair Housing Seminars
The first matter relates to funding provided within the city’s annual budget for
Community Development Block Grant (“CDBG”) funds. CDBG funds are federal funds
allocated to the city annually. The Department ofHousing and Urban Development (“JIUD”)
determines the amount received by the city. Federal regulations limit the purposes for which
such funds may be used.


File No. A-99-211
Page3
After the city receives notice of its allocation, it budgets the CDBG funds for various
purposes consistent with federal regulations. This budget includes funds to hire an outside
person or agency to provide fair housing seminars open to the public. While the funding is
approved as a line item within the CDBG budget, the city council does not select the provider of
the service. However, the narrative accompanying the budget notes that city staff intends to
contract with Legal Services for that service. This contract is ordinarily executed on behalfof
Legal Services by its managing attorney, who is Councilmember Jarvis. However, it could be
executed by other Legal Services personnel.
Community Organization Funding by the City
The second matter relates to the city’s community organization funding. The city
annually distributes funds to nonprofit organizations that provide services to city residents.
These funds are derived from the federal CDBG allocation and the city’s general fund. Once the
city council decides on the total amount of funding to be made available, applications are
solicited. The city council then reviews each application to determine which organizations it will
fund, and how much they will receive. This decision is made without regard to whether the
individual organizations will receive money from the general fund or CDBG funds, a
determination later made by city staff. After the city council approves the funding, city staff first
assigns CDBG money to those organizations for which such funding is appropriate, and then
allocates general funds to the remainder ofthe organizations.
Legal Services annually submits an application for funding for the Chico branch office.
Councilmember Jarvis, in her position as managing attorney, reviews and initially approves that
application. It is then forwarded to the executive office in Sacramento for final review, possible
changes and submission to the city. The annual amount requested by Legal Services in its
application is approximately $20,000, about 3.5 percent of the budget for the Chico office. The
funding application includes amounts specifically to be used to fund a portion ofthe salaries for
specified employees, including the salary of Councilmember Jarvis. The amount requested to be
used for the managing attorney’s salary is approximately $5,600. However, it is possible to
reallocate how city funds will be spent so that they do not contribute to her salary. The
councilmember’s salary from Legal Services is not contingent upon the city council’s decision to
fund Legal Services. The agreement between the city and Legal Services governing the grant of
such funding is ordinarily executed on behalf of Legal Services by its managing attorney.
However, it can be executed by other Legal Services staff.
ANALYSIS
The Act prohibits public officials from making, participating in making, or in any way
attempting to use their official position to influence a governmental decision in which they have
a financial interest. (Section 87100.) As a member ofthe Chico City Council, Ms. Jarvis is a
public official for purposes ofthe Act. (Section 82048.)


File No. A-99-21 1
Page 4
Making, Participating in Making, and Influencing Governmental Decisions
The prohibition in Section 87100 applies to specific conduct: making, participating in
making, or using one’s official position to iufluence a governmental decision. (Section 87100;
Regulations 18702.1-18702.4.) Thus, the Act does not prohibit the councilmember from signing
or executing any contract or agreement on behalf of Legal Services. However, other laws outside
of the Act, such as Government Code Section 1090, might restrict her ability to serve in a public
office while simultaneously contracting with the agency in a private capacity. Since the
Commission’s jurisdiction is limited to the Act, we cannot address such laws. (Section 83111.)
Identifying Economic Interests
An official has a financial interest in a decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the
decision will have a material financial effect, distinguishable from its effect on the public
generally, on the official, or on, among other enumerated economic interests, any source of
income of $250 or more provided to the official within 12 months prior to the time the decision
is made. (Section 87103(c).)
Councilmember Jarvis is employed by Legal Services as the managing attorney of one of
its branch offices. You indicate that her income from Legal Services has exceeded $250 within
the past 12 months. Therefore, she has an economic interest in Legal Services, as defined in
Section 87103. Accordingly, she may not make, participate in making, or use her official
position to influence any decision if it is reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a
material financial effect on Legal Services.
Is it reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on
her economic interest?
Once she identifies her economic interest, the councilmember must evaluate whether it is
reasonably foreseeable that the decision will have a material financial effect on that economic
interest. First, she must determine whether the economic interest will be directly or indirectly
involved in the decision. (Regulation 18700(b)(4).) Based upon the type of involvement, she
must then locate the appropriate materiality standard. (Regulation 18700(b)(5).) Afler finding
the relevant materiality standard, she must decide whether it is reasonably foreseeable that the
materiality standard will be met. (Regulation 18700(b)(6).)
1. Direct or Indirect Involvement
A source of income is directly involved in a decision if the entity initiates, is a named
party in, or the subject of, the decision. (Regulation 18704.1(a)(2).) An entity is the “subject of
a decision” if the decision involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any
license, permit or contract with the person. (Id.) Legal Services is directly involved in the city’s


File No. A-99-211
Page 5
decision to approve CDBG funding for fair housing seminars, which the city council knows will
result in a contract between the city and Legal Services. Legal Services is also directly involved
in the city’s decision to allocate public funds to Legal Services.
2. Applicable Materiality Standard
When a nonprofit entity that is a source of income is directly involved in a decision, the
pertinent materiality standard is set forth in Regulation 18705.3(a). Under that standard,any
reasonably foreseeable financial effect on the nonprofit entity is deemed to be material.
Pursuant to a special rule, known as the “nexus test,” any reasonably foreseeable financial
effect of a decision on a source of income is deemed to be material ifthe public official receives
income to achieve a goal or purpose that would be furthered or defeated by the decision.
(Regulation 18705.3(c).) This special materiality rule might be implicated here. However, we
do not address it since we have afready determined that the same materiality threshold—any
reasonably foreseeable financial effect—afready applies to your facts.
3. Is it reasonably foreseeable that the materiality standard will be met?
An effect is considered reasonably foreseeable ifthere is a substantial likelihood that it
will occur. (Regulation 18706.) A material financial effect need not be a certainty as a result of
the decision, but it must be more than a mere possibility. When the effect of a decision is
deemed to be material under the relevant materiality standard, as in this case, the official must
determine whether it is substantially likely that any financial effect will occur as a result of the
decision. (Regulation 1 8700(b)(6).) This is a “one-penny” rule—if any financial effect is
reasonably foreseeable, the official will have a disqualif’ing financial interest in the decision.
Under the relevant materiality standard, any reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
Legal Services as a result ofthe city’s decisions is deemed to be material. (Regulation
18705.3(a).) Therefore, unless the two funding decisions described above will have no financial
effect whatsoever on Legal Services—an unlikely outcome—Councilmember Jarvis is
disqualified from both decisions.
Segmentation
The first decision concerns a line item within the CDBG budget providing funding for
fair housing seminars. Although Councilmember Jarvis may not participate with respect to that
budgetary item, it might be possible for her to participate on other aspects ofthe budget. Often a
public body may divide large and complex decisions, like budget decisions, into separate
decisions in order to allow a public official, who has a conflict in one component, to participate
in the other parts of the decision. The Commission has devised a procedure that must be
followed if the councilmember wishes to provide input onother items in the budget:


File No. A-99-21 1
Page 6
The line item in which she has a conflict must be considered separately.
That item must be considered first, and a final decision must be reached with regard to
that decision.
Once a decision has been made on that item, Councilmember Jarvis may participate in the
deliberations regarding the other budget items, as long as those deliberations do not
reopen or in any way affect the item in which she has a conflict. (Huffaker Advice Letter,
No. A-86-343.)
In addition, the councilmember may also vote on the adoption of the final budget, even if
the final budget includes the item from which she was disqualified, provided that the decision
does not reopen or in any way alter the decision from which she was disqualified. (Cook Advice
Letter, No. A-83-163.)
Please note that this segmentation analysis does not apply to the second decision
described in your request. In some cases, a series of decisions may be too interrelated to be
considered separately. In your request, you stated that after the city determines the total amount
of funding to be allocated to community service organizations, it then decides which
organizations it will fund, and how much each will receive. Consequently, a decision regarding
one organization will affect the amount of funds available to the others. These decisions are
therefore too interlinked to be decided separately. As such, Councilmember Jarvis has a conflict
of interest with regard to each application. (For a similar discussion regarding segmentation, see
the Epp Advice Letter, No. A-97-l00, copy enclosed.)
If you have other questions regarding this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.
Sincerely,
Steven G. Churchwell
General Counsel
Staff Counsel, Legal Division

Enclosure
By:
